g.data-narration: Welcome to CPU Scheduling - the fundamental mechanism that allows your computer to run multiple programs efficiently.
CPU scheduling is the OS's decision of which process runs on the CPU at any time, ensuring efficient use of the processor.
g.text: In multiprogramming, multiple processes reside in memory but CPU executes one instruction at a time.
The scheduler selects which process runs next from the ready queue.

g.data-narration: The main goals are maximizing CPU utilization and throughput while minimizing turnaround, waiting, and response times.
We measure scheduling performance using five key metrics: CPU utilization, throughput, turnaround, waiting, and response time.
Processes move between queues: Job Queue (all processes), Ready Queue (waiting for CPU), and Device Queues (waiting for I/O).
Three scheduler types exist: Long-term (controls multiprogramming), Short-term (fast CPU allocation), and Medium-term (swapping).
Context switch saves the running process state to its PCB, then loads the new process state - pure overhead with no useful work.
g.text: Pure overhead - no useful work done

g.data-narration: Preemptive scheduling allows interrupting running processes; non-preemptive runs until termination or waiting state.
g.text: Runs to completion or waiting state
Circumstances: 1) I/O request, 2) Interrupt, 3) I/O completion, 4) Termination

g.data-narration: FCFS serves processes in arrival order. Simple but suffers from convoy effect - short processes wait behind long ones.
g.text: Processes: P1(24ms), P2(3ms), P3(3ms) arriving at time 0
Convoy Effect: Short processes wait behind long process

g.data-narration: SJF selects shortest burst first - optimal for average waiting time but impossible to predict actual burst length accurately.
g.text: Processes: P1(6), P2(8), P3(7), P4(3) - all arrive at time 0
Provably optimal for minimizing average waiting time

g.data-narration: Priority scheduling allocates CPU to highest priority process but can cause starvation - solved by aging to boost waiting process priorities.
Round Robin uses time quantum - each process gets equal CPU time. Performance depends on quantum size: large becomes FCFS, small causes overhead.
g.text: Time Quantum = 4ms | Processes: P1(24), P2(3), P3(3)

g.data-narration: Each algorithm has trade-offs: FCFS simple but poor performance, SJF optimal but unpredictable, RR fair but overhead-heavy.
Thread scheduling has two scopes: PCS (user-level thread scheduling) and SCS (kernel-level thread scheduling).
Remember: waiting time equals turnaround minus burst time, draw Gantt charts for calculations, and SJF is optimal but impractical.
